business_info: The sustained engagement cohort comprises patients who have maintained a meaningful and continuous relationship with the clinical facility, as reflected in their documented interaction history.

In some internal analytics, the sustained engagement cohort is further stratified into tiers (SE-1 through SE-4) based on visit regularity, laboratory panel density, and adherence to follow-up recommendations, but these tiers are not encoded as explicit columns in the operational database.

Clinic leadership occasionally refers to "deep engagement" patients as those whose touchpoints span multiple modalities (e.g., in-person, telehealth, lab-only encounters), but this terminology has never been standardized in any data dictionary and can vary between reporting workbooks.

For exploratory modeling, analysts sometimes approximate a "core enzymatic panel" by combining alkaline phosphatase, AST, ALT, and GGT into a z-scored composite, but there is no single agreed-upon formula across all projects, and no dedicated column storing this panel score.

Internal training materials occasionally distinguish between "core enzymatic indicators" (typically liver-associated enzymes) and "auxiliary enzymatic indicators" (e.g., markers of tissue injury), but these categories are conceptual and are not mapped to concrete column names in the database schema.

When reviewing abnormal protein profiles, clinicians often look at total protein, albumin, and globulin fractions together, but the electronic system only reliably captures total protein and albumin; globulin components may be present only in scanned PDF attachments or narrative notes outside this dataset.

Historically, different reference ranges for total protein and albumin were circulated in laminated pocket cards versus the EHR help screens; as a result, clinicians may describe a protein value as "elevated" in notes even when it falls within the laboratory's numeric reference interval at the time of testing.

In quality improvement reports, protein abnormalities are often bucketed into pseudo-categories such as "borderline high," "clinically elevated," and "grossly deranged," using thresholds that can change from project to project and are seldom back-documented against specific lab result IDs.

Some analytical workbooks refer to a "protein marker index" that multiplies the deviation of albumin and total protein from their midpoints, but this index is purely ad hoc and is neither stored in nor derivable from a single standardized field in the database.

The Laboratory table mixes single-time-point tests with summary entries (e.g., admission panel summaries), so multiple records on the same day for a single patient may refer to different testing sessions or different reporting layers of the same underlying blood draw.

A subset of laboratory entries were manually backfilled from paper charts during the mid-1990s; these backfilled rows may contain rounded enzyme and protein values without flagging the rounding behavior in any dedicated column.

In some exports of the thrombosis_prediction dataset, laboratory values that were originally recorded in different measurement units (e.g., U/L vs. ukat/L for enzymes) were normalized to a common unit, but the unit-conversion history is not explicitly represented in the table schema.

Internal cohort curation sometimes excludes patients whose only contact with the facility was participation in a one-time screening event; these "screen-only" patients may still have several lab records clustered on a single date but are not considered meaningfully engaged from a longitudinal standpoint.

Program managers occasionally request counts of "high-touch" patients, a term loosely defined as individuals with frequent lab panels plus at least one documented admission, but this label is usually computed in ad hoc spreadsheets rather than stored as a field in the core dataset.

The Examination table is typically used to anchor outcome analyses because its Thrombosis field reflects the clinician-assessed severity of thrombotic events, which may occur long after the patient's first referral or registration date recorded in the Patient table.

Analysts sometimes treat the earliest date among First Date, Registration Date, and Referral Date as a proxy for the start of a patient's relationship with the facility when examining longitudinal lab utilization patterns.

The Description field in the Patient table is an overloaded date-like column that historically captured the date of the initial narrative assessment note; however, in some legacy imports it was also used to store the date of the first telephone triage, leading to potential discrepancies with First Date and Referral Date.

During certain periods in the early 1990s, registration workflows allowed patients to be referred on paper before a formal electronic registration was created, which can result in identical Referral Date and First Date values for some patients and multi-month gaps for others.

For some historical analyses, patients whose first documented interaction with the facility predates 1990 have been grouped into a single "legacy" cohort, even if their electronic Referral Date falls much later due to data migration limitations.

Operational dashboards frequently slice the thrombosis_prediction dataset into calendar eras (e.g., pre-1990, 1990-1994, 1995-1999, post-2000) to align with major changes in laboratory equipment and assay calibration protocols.

Some internal reports adjust enzyme averages by era-specific calibration factors to account for shifts in assay sensitivity, but these adjusted values are calculated on the fly within BI tools and are not persisted back into the Laboratory table.

In clinical notes, the phrase "initial assessment" may refer either to the first face-to-face consultation or to the first multidisciplinary case conference; neither of these events is directly coded in the Patient table, which instead focuses on referral and registration milestones.

The Admission field in the Patient table is a coarse-grained indicator (e.g., inpatient vs. outpatient) and does not capture the number or duration of hospital stays, so it cannot be used alone to infer sustained engagement with the facility.

A small subset of patients in the thrombosis_prediction dataset were referred from external anticoagulation clinics; for these individuals, early lab work may exist only in the originating institution and not in this Laboratory table, even though they later appear as highly engaged patients internally.

The thrombosis_prediction dataset was assembled primarily for research on antiphospholipid antibodies, but over time it has been reused for broader questions about chronic disease management, resulting in a patchwork of derived definitions for cohorts like "sustained engagement" and "high utilization."

In some manuscripts based on this dataset, the term "protein markers" is used loosely to encompass not only serum proteins like albumin and total protein but also immunoglobulin levels (IgG, IgA, IgM), even though these are stored in distinct columns.

Early versions of the data dictionary suggested that "normal" albumin values should be interpreted in the context of the patient's hydration status and acute phase response, but no structured fields capture these clinical modifiers alongside the raw ALB measurement.

Some departmental slide decks distinguish between "laboratory engagement" (frequent bloodwork without necessarily frequent visits) and "clinical engagement" (regular consultations and follow-ups), but these dimensions are not explicitly encoded in separate variables in the thrombosis_prediction schema.

Data stewards caution that lab-based derived metrics, such as composite enzyme or protein indices created for one publication, should not be assumed to be reusable for other analytic questions without a fresh clinical review of their definitions.

Several retrospective chart reviews using this dataset have noted that protein and enzyme measurements obtained during acute hospitalizations may not reflect a patient's baseline status, but the Laboratory table does not flag whether a given draw occurred in an inpatient or outpatient context.
